content:
I don't think we need two separate chapters here, because Chapter 2 ( https://www.codingame.com/contribute/view/1457433ef751d8be85463ed5048fbf6dc459d6 ) naturally generalises Chapter 1.

From an implementation perspective, the only additional element when solving Chapter 2 is maintaining a running value and applying the gates in sequence. Only minimal modifications to a solution for Chapter 1 would be needed to solve Chapter 2. Because of this, keeping them as separate puzzles risks adding redundancy rather than additional learning value.

Since you mentioned that the plan is to have five chapters in the series, it'd be helpful to know what the other chapter will involve.

Also note that we already have a couple of logic-gate related puzzles on the platform, so it would be good to ensure the new series is clearly differentiated from them.

Existing puzzle 1 - Logic Gates ( https://www.codingame.com/training/easy/logic-gates ): Quite similar to your Chapter 2, except that the inputs and outputs are signal strings (series of 0s and 1s), and each logic gate operates across the entire strings instead of single 0/1 values.

Existing puzzle 2 - Logic Gates Detective ( https://www.codingame.com/training/medium/logic-gates-detective ): An extension of your Chapter 2: besides evaluating gates, it also requires deducing unknown inputs from known outputs, and resolving dependency rules in a to-be-determined order rather than the given order.

Other points to note when creating the series:

* As mentioned above, a puzzle named "Logic gates" already exists. Please use a different name for your series to avoid confusion.

* Use the "Logic" tag only. Using multiple tags (Logic, Logic Gates, Logical problem, Logic Chapters) is redundant.

* Please remember to format the different sections using the syntax <<bold>>, [[variable]] and {{constant}}.

* The constraints should closely reflect the test cases. For example, there is a mismatch if the upper bound of [[N]] is 100 while the largest value used in the test cases is only 5.

* Consider designing a cover image for the whole puzzle series.

There is no need to rush into creating the other chapters in the series. Spending some time planning the overall structure, and making sure the first chapter is well-designed and can serve as a template, will make the creation of the remaining chapters much smoother and less time-consuming.

For the time being, let me know the planned details of the remaining chapters.

Thanks.

I'll review Chapters 1 and 2 within the next few days. There will likely be some changes that apply to all of these puzzles, so it might be best to wait for the comments before starting on the other parts. Otherwise, you may end up needing to make a lot of changes later on.
lol 🤣😂 I just realised that I made this in like an hour after i submitted another of my contributions :)
Feel free to give comments, as this will be part of an ongoing 5 part series of logic gate problems!
thanks! it's kinda nice having ya around cause I'm new to this contribution thing :)
I've tried doing what you've asked, but I'm not a very good designer for these cover images... -_- do you have any recommendations for like generally designing these cover images?

Cover images don't need to be anything fancy. Even something very simple is perfectly fine. For example, you could use a word processor or a spreadsheet program to arrange a few text boxes or shapes, and that would already work well enough.

More importantly, it would be helpful if you could first share the planned details for the remaining chapters in the series. Since the same cover image can be used for the entire series, having a clearer idea of the overall plan may also make it easier to decide what kind of cover image would fit best.

Thank you.

So the original plan was:

1. simple logic gates, introduce AND/OR, and etc etc
2. logic gates simple problems requiring probably 2 -3 logic gates
3. logic problems that require 3+ logic gates
4. ADDERS, and 4-bit, 8-bit. something like that
5. still thinking...

Thank you for sharing the plan.

Chapters 1 to 3 sound quite similar. In practice, they may end up being variations of the same task, simply with more gates involved. Because of that, it's better to combine Chapters 1 to 3 into a single puzzle that evaluates a sequence of logic gates.

Chapter 4: The idea of adders feels quite different from simply evaluating gates, so this could work as a separate puzzle on its own.

In my opinion, a full series is probably unnecessary. Creating one or two well-designed puzzles could be clearer and more distinct than stretching the idea into five chapters.

what if i introduce a new input to consider in chapters 1-3? 
like assuming i add a termination command or a looping command...

I would need a bit more detail about how the new input would work before commenting properly. However, if the change only requires a few small modifications to an existing solution in order to handle the new command, that alone probably wouldn't justify separating the ideas into multiple chapters.

so i was thinking, if terminate was printed into the script then stop the process of the current logic gate that is being operated, then continue with the rest.

if LOOP 3, then repeat this action for 3 times.

any other ideas? i feel like i need to introduce something to make other chapters different, however i will consider making this a trilogy instead if there's good reason to.
